digest.top_line: ReCOG/ESTRO/ASTRO consensus: 17-member panel guidance on definitive + postop reirradiation for recurrent HNSCC in prior RT fields; evidence mostly low-level, role 'remains undefined'.
digest.tldr: Head-neck carried the only signal: a multi-society reirradiation consensus (6 rad-oncs drafting, 17-member ratifying panel) across six domains, from pt selection to dose accumulation across prior + reRT to toxicity. Guidance, not new efficacy data; the evidence base stays low-level and heterogeneous, so it frames practice rather than changes it.

sites.intro: Recurrent or second-primary HNSCC within a prior RT field has few options; this consensus standardizes when and how to reirradiate.

studies.name: ReCOG/ESTRO/ASTRO Reirradiation Consensus
studies.tldr: International expert consensus on definitive and postoperative reirradiation for recurrent HNSCC within previously irradiated fields; ratified by a 17-member rad-onc panel.
details: 🔗 15-50% of HNSCC pts recur or develop a second primary; few options within a prior RT field
🔍 Core drafting group: 6 radiation oncologists, 3 physicists, 1 research fellow
🔍 Ratified by a 17-member international rad-onc panel; single formal vote, no-opinion kept in denominator, no re-vote

details.text: 📐 Oxford CEBM evidence level tagged per statement

details: 🔍 Modern conformal techniques cited: IMRT, VMAT, proton therapy, SBRT (therapeutic ratio improved over past 20 yrs)
⚠️ Excludes nasopharyngeal carcinoma (2021 international recs) and rare histologies
⚠️ Evidence mostly low-level and heterogeneous; role of modern reirradiation 'remains undefined'
studies.significance: The additive value is a shared framework where evidence sits mostly at level 3-4: it formalizes patient selection, cumulative-dose accounting across the prior course, and target delineation. Salvage surgery stays first-line where feasible, definitive or postoperative reRT otherwise. It standardizes how you plan and consent a reirradiation candidate, not whether reRT works.
studies.monday_clinic: In a pt with recurrent or second-primary HNSCC arising within a previously irradiated field (nasopharynx and rare histologies excluded), this consensus informs structured selection for definitive or postoperative reirradiation; it does not extend to nasopharyngeal recurrence.
open_questions: Prospective randomised trials of modern reirradiation efficacy
Optimal cumulative dose constraints across reirradiation courses
Patient selection criteria predicting reirradiation benefit

related_trials.brief_title: SBRT +/- Pembrolizumab in Patients With Local-Regionally Recurrent or Second Primary Head and Neck Carcinoma
related_trials.brief_summary: This phase II trial with a safety run-in component will evaluate whether the addition of pembrolizumab to Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT) re-irradiation will improve the progression-free survival for patients with recurrent or new second primary Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma (HNSCC).

related_trials.brief_title: Re-Radiochemotherapy and Pembrolizumab vs. Immuno(Chemo)Therapy for Locoregionally Recurrent PD-L1 Positive (CPS≥1) HNSCC

studies.name: RTOG 1112
studies.tldr: SBRT added to sorafenib: mOS 15.8 vs 12.3mo (HR 0.77, 1-sided p=.06); adjusted HR 0.72 p=.04; mPFS 9.2 vs 5.5mo.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT, 193 randomized (177 eligible), SBRT→sorafenib vs sorafenib alone; MVI 74%, 85% male, median age 66
🔍 SBRT personalized 27.5-50 Gy/5fx; pts unsuitable for or refractory to standard local-regional therapy
⚠️ Prespecified 1° OS analysis (unadjusted, 1-sided) missed at p=.06; significance emerged only after adjusting for stratification
⚠️ Accrual stopped early for SOC change; sorafenib now superseded (atezo/bev 1L) → benefit on a modern backbone unknown
📊 G3+ TRAEs 47% (SBRT+sorafenib) vs 42% (sorafenib), p=.52; adding SBRT did not raise grade-3 toxicity
📊 6-mo QoL improved in 35% (6/17) SBRT arm vs 10% (2/20) sorafenib; small N

verdict.rationale: Randomized phase 3 arguing RT adds benefit over systemic-only, but prespecified unadjusted OS primary narrowly missed (adjusted analysis hit); accrual stopped early, sorafenib comparator now obsolete.
verdict.audience: Locally advanced HCC w/ macrovascular invasion, 1L systemic candidate

studies.significance: The RT case rests on PFS, not the narrowly-missed OS primary: adding SBRT (27.5-50 Gy/5fx) nearly doubled mPFS (9.2 vs 5.5mo, HR 0.55) and improved 6-mo QoL (35% vs 10%) with no added G3+ toxicity (47 vs 42%). Supports layering SBRT onto systemic therapy in MVI-heavy locally advanced HCC, though the sorafenib backbone predates atezo/bev.
studies.monday_clinic: In locally advanced HCC with macrovascular invasion who are first-line systemic candidates unsuitable for standard local-regional therapy, this supports adding liver SBRT to systemic therapy; it does not speak to SBRT layered on modern IO doublets, since the sorafenib comparator is now dated.
open_questions: SBRT benefit with modern IO systemic backbone vs obsolete sorafenib
Which HCC subgroup gains most from added SBRT

related_trials.brief_title: Atezolizumab Plus Bevacizumab Alone or Combined with External Beam Radiotherapy for HCC with Macrovascular Invasion
related_trials.brief_summary:
The recent global IMbrave150 study evaluated the combination of atezolizumab and bevacizumab versus sorafenib in 501 patients with advanced or metastatic Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC). The median overall survival (OS) was notably better in the atezolizumab/bevacizumab group. However, for HCC patients with intrahepatic macrovascular invasion (MVI), the prognosis remains poor, indicating a significant unmet need in this group.

External Beam Radiotherapy (EBRT) has shown promising results in…

papers.title: Reirradiation for recurrent head and neck squamous cell carcinoma: international expert consensus recommendations endorsed by the Reirradiation Collaborative Group, the European Society for Radiotherapy and Oncology Reirradiation Focus Group, and the American Society for Radiation Oncology
Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy vs Sorafenib Alone in Hepatocellular Carcinoma: The NRG Oncology/RTOG 1112 Phase 3 Randomized Clinical Trial.
papers.abstract:
IMPORTANCE: Most patients with locally advanced hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) recur within the liver following systemic therapy.

OBJECTIVE: To determine whether stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) improves outcomes in patients with locally advanced HCC compared with sorafenib alone.

DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS: This multicenter phase 3 randomized clinical trial randomized patients with HCC 1:1 to sorafenib or SBRT followed by sorafenib, stratified by performance status, liver function, degree of metastases, and macrovascular invasion. Eligible patients had HCC unsuitable for or refractory to standard local-regional therapies and were candidates for first-line systemic therapy. Data were collected from April 2013 to March 2021, and data were analyzed from July 2022 to August 2023.

INTERVENTION: Personalized SBRT, 27.5 to 50 Gy in 5 fractions.

MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES: The primary end point was overall survival (OS). Secondary end points were progression-free survival (PFS), adverse events, and quality of life.

RESULTS: Of 193 patients randomized, 177 were eligible. Accrual was stopped early due to a change in standard-of-care systemic therapy. Of 177 included patients, 150 (84.7%) were male, and the median (IQR) age was 66 (60-72) years. Macrovascular invasion was seen in 131 (74.0%). As of July 1, 2022, the median OS was 12.3 months (90% CI, 10.6-14.3) with sorafenib vs 15.8 months (90% CI, 11.4-19.2) following SBRT and sorafenib (hazard ratio [HR], 0.77; 90% CI, 0.59-1.01; 1-sided P = .06). Adjusting for stratification factors, OS was improved with SBRT (HR, 0.72; 95% CI, 0.52-0.99; 2-sided P = .04). Median PFS was improved from 5.5 months (95% CI, 3.4-6.3) with sorafenib to 9.2 months (95% CI, 7.5-11.9) with SBRT and sorafenib (HR, 0.55; 95% CI, 0.40-0.75; 2-sided P < .001). Treatment-related grade 3 or higher adverse events were seen in 37 of 88 (42%) and 39 of 83 (47%) of patients treated with sorafenib vs SBRT and sorafenib, respectively (P = .52). There were 2 treatment-related deaths in the sorafenib group (death not otherwise specified and liver failure) and 1 in the SBRT and sorafenib group (lung infection). At 6 months, improved quality of life was seen in 2 of 20 (10%) and 6 of 17 (35%) of patients treated with sorafenib and SBRT and sorafenib, respectively.

CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE: In this phase 3 randomized clinical trial, among patients with locally advanced HCC, SBRT was associated with a clinically important but not statistically significant improved overall survival compared with sorafenib alone.

TRIAL REGISTRATION: ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: NCT01730937.
